text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully dissect thick adhesions from the inferior edge of the gallbladder. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence highlights the gallbladder wall, aiding in identifying dissection planes. The surgeon frees adhesions at the gallbladder's bottom, aiming to access the hepatocystic triangle for duct and artery dissection. The common duct and cystic duct are visible amidst the distorted anatomy.

The surgeon attempts to dissect the bottom of the gallbladder near the cystic duct, encountering a thick layer of adhesions in the peritoneum. The gallbladder is elevated, tenting the common bile duct, and causing discomfort with the dissection. A small amount of bleeding occurs, prompting the surgeon to switch to a top-down approach. Scarring from a previous tube insertion complicates the dissection into the liver bed. A sponge is used to control the bleeding and maintain a dry surgical field.

The surgeon continues to dissect the gallbladder from the liver using a top-down approach, aided by robotic instruments. ICG imaging helps visualize the gallbladder wall and liver bed, guiding the dissection to minimize bleeding. The surgeon isolates the cystic duct and artery, encountering and controlling feeding vessels with bipolar cautery. The junction of the cystic duct to the common duct is visible, and the surgeon works to open the peritoneum at the gallbladder's base to further isolate the cystic duct.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the cystic duct, identifying the cystic artery higher in the triangle. Using ICG for enhanced visualization, the surgeon clears the back of the cystic duct and identifies a crossing hepatic artery. A window is created behind the cystic artery, followed by clipping the artery and duct, ensuring hemostasis to conclude the procedure.
